Good afternoon everybody. Welcome to the Diamond Members Call. I have as promised with me today Rick Cesare from the Infomercial and Direct Response Marketing World, a world he and I share. I have recommended his book to you. If you have not already obtained it, I'm sure by the end of this call you will make the decision to do so. And it is, as they say available at all fine booksellers, this very moment, the title of which is by now Creative Marketing that gets customers to respond to you and to your product. I've given you in print a more elaborate introduction, but I will remind you that Rick is the brain or half of the brain, in some cases, integrally involved with some of the biggest brand built by direct response of our time. The juicepan, the George Foreman grill, oxy fresh, sonic toothbrush, etc. We'll be talking about some of those. I do want to quickly make the point before we get started that I fully recognize that the majority of you on the call may not be about taking some idea, invention, product, some part of your business and making it a national or global direct response item. But I would remind you that direct response is direct response is direct response and whether you're running a local brick and mortar business, retail business, service business, professional practice, or operating on a broader scale, the same principles apply in many cases you're selling to the exact same consumers and in many cases you are or should be making the same kind of message in creative cases that somebody like Rick would make on a global scale for a giant marketing campaign for a product such as the George Foreman grill. Many of you, of course, by the way, met George when he was at the Info Summit and probably, I think it's now, Rick might know, it's either one out of five or one out of four households in America have one of those puppies. So the odds are you probably see that thing when you walk into your kitchen. So anyway, Rick, welcome and appreciate you being on the call and taking the time with us. And I'll start right out with, I have a handful of questions before we let them ask you questions. Sure. At first I want to say thank you very much, Dan, for the opportunity to do this. I really appreciate it. I've been a big fan of yours. There aren't too many people that go back as far as we have in this industry and have always been a big fan of yours and your books and your work. And I have to be honest, I've used a lot of your ideas and techniques and some of the things I did and in the introduction you gave, it is very important while some of these products have become very big global brands, almost all of them started as an idea with very little sales and it was being able to sell to just a few people and then take the techniques of the infomercial to really magnify that and help them grow. But at the end of the day, the selling techniques and the direct response techniques, if they don't work on a one-on-one or a small business level, they won't work on a large level either. Yeah, and that's a great point. And I do, I'll sort of jump then to what would have been my second question. So a point in case to all that is what you did with Jay and the Juice Man. And so I want to ask you about that so people really get a handle on how these things start. And as you point out, the most intelligent among us don't start them by blowing a half a million dollars on a TV campaign or a radio campaign or for that matter a print campaign. We sort of prove that we have something we can sell in some other venues. So give folks a sense of where the Juice Man wound up. And then if you will talk a little bit about the beginnings on radio with free recipe and the beginnings in the seminar environment. Sure. At the end of the time, when we started the Juice Man business in 1989, in three and a half years we grew the sales from zero to 75 million. And then we sold it to a company in Chicago called Salton. And Salton later was a company that did the George Forman Grill. But we'll talk about that later. But the way I found out about Jay Kordich, I attended a home show in Seattle. And if you've ever been to a small local trade show, there's a lot of booths, you know, 10 by 10 booths and people are selling products. Well, this one booth, all the booths had like one or two people around them and this one booth had 50 people around them. And Jay was there talking about juice extractors and he really had captivated the audience. And so I asked if I could speak with him after the show and he agreed. And we started talking about what he was doing and he was going around the country at these state fairs, home shows, selling anywhere from one to 20 juicers at a time based on his appearances at these venues. And so I thought that I had been in the direct response field at the time and thought that we could take this and take what he was doing on a small level and then build it into something bigger. But it was interesting the way we started. We didn't go out and make an infomercial right away to what your comments earlier. Basically, the first thing we did was Jay is a captivating personality and he's a good pitch man. He's good at selling his products. So what we did was we called different television and TV stations around the country. We couldn't afford when we started this to actually hire a PR firm yet. And there was a company, a television station we got lucky with in New York. At the time it was WOR. And again, this goes back to 1989 and to kind of give you an idea, Matt Lauer was the local host at the time who's now obviously well known on Good Morning America. But anyway, Jay appeared on that WOR show. It's funny because it's almost the anniversary of it coming up. It was June 30th and he was on a 20-minute segment and basically told people that they could, if they sent us an envelope with a dollar that he would send them a list of recipes. So this was right before the July 4th holiday and we didn't hear anything for a while. I heard from the TV station that their switchboard was lit up. There was a lot of response. But it was before the Internet. So everything happened through the mail. And on July 6th, the post truck pulls up to our office and the postman gets out and he's got a big, I don't know if you've ever seen the big canvas, mail sacks. He has one of those over his shoulder and he comes in and brings it in and I go, wow, what's all that? And he goes, I don't know, you've got a lot of mail today. He goes back out to the truck and he ends up bringing in three more sacks of envelopes. We ended up getting over 12,000 responses from that one media appearance that Jay did. And basically what we did is take all the names and addresses of the people that wrote in and we basically sent them back out. I hastily printed up flyer to sell the juicers and that's what launched the business, that one appearance. But it was still quite a while before we actually made an infomercial. We used that technique of putting Jay on radio and TV appearances and having people send us an envelope for free recipes and that's how we kind of built the base of the business before we actually started doing Direct Response television. And it's still something that I use to this day. PR is a very powerful way if you have a good person and it's even more effective today because you use the PR to drive people to a website and then sell your product. So if you have a good product, service, spokesperson and a way to get on radio and TV, that's a really, really effective way to either start a business or supplement other types of marketing that you're doing and it works on both local level and national level. You also, if I remember correctly, you also used media and filled seminar rooms, right? Right. Yeah, because that was my background before getting, that's how I kind of learned the Direct Response industry, the first people, and I don't know if it was the first, but among the first people to use infomercials were all the real estate guys, you know, Robert Allen, Albert Lowry, how to buy real estate with no money down, this was the mid-80s, and that was my background. I was in real estate before getting into the direct marketing and had successfully promoted real estate seminars through direct mail and then through television. And so before actually selling direct to consumer with the infomercial, we basically made a 30-minute TV show and we would go to a city and we would run the half-hour infomercial and ask people to show up at different ballrooms of local hotels and we supplemented that with the PR. So if we went to Houston, for example, we would run local television time, ask people to show up for free seminars, they'd be a 90-minute lecture about the program at the end of the time that people could order juice machines, which we would then ship out of our office in Seattle, and all those seminars were supplemented with local PR as well. And the PR acts as a...people are skeptical sometimes, or most of the times, when they see just advertising and it's always, you know, a challenge to get people to respond to your ads. When you layer in the PR and the media is talking about it, it adds a level of credibility then that works synergistically with the advertising and the two work together very well. And I would point out to people that, you know, we went all the way back to kind of the oldest of your case histories and we're talking 80s and 90s and yet really nothing in terms of the architecture of all this has really changed much and there's no reason not to use any and all of these methods in today's world. Just now the internet media gives us some additional weapons to integrate with them, but we might very well take somebody to market this very same way today. Absolutely. The model itself and still works and the only thing that all the additional, whether it's social media, whether it's the internet, all those things just give you more marketing tools, but the same basic principles and methods are the same whether it goes back over 25 years with the original Juice Man to our most current project which is absolutely internet-based. I don't know how many people listening are familiar with the GoPro cameras. They're the little cameras that you see the skiers and snowboarders wear on their helmets. That's a company that has used kind of the latest techniques, but it's to grow. They were on the cover of Inc. Magazine a couple months ago. They grew from 0 to 250 million in under three years and basically the same marketing model we used 25 years ago just adapted to the new technology was responsible for part of that success. The second question that I have for you is one, so you probably most of the time people who come to you think they have the next best thing to slice bread and that they have something really cool and in some cases as with the camera they do. What I hear more often, which is what I want to ask you about, is from people who think that what their business is about is really very ordinary and they will struggle mightily trying to find their way to a unique selling proposition and sometimes insist that there's nothing unique about what they do. They grumble about being commoditized and price suppressed as a result of being commoditized as well. I consistently put examples in front of them of things that couldn't be more mundane and ordinary that are made into something more than that. Your business is of course largely about that. The camera is almost an exception to the rule. When you think about a countertop grill or essentially a blender or a toothbrush it's hard to be more commoditized in many ways than that and yet you're able to take them to TV or take them to other media and you're able to build multi-million dollar brands out of them. So I wonder if you would just talk a little bit about the approach to an ordinary product, a toothbrush for example, and finding a way to present it as something extraordinary. Yeah, that's a really important point you talked about the unique selling proposition. We'll get to talk more about the camera later because there's nothing special about this camera except the mounts. Meanwhile, this company is growing dramatically and Kodak is going out of business so go bankrupt. But go back to answer your question. You always have to kind of look at products a different way and I'll start with the juicer and then we'll get into the toothbrush and then the grill because at the time there were lots of juicers being sold in the country when we started this in 1989. But the companies that were selling them were appliance manufacturers and the products were being marketed as appliances, as a kitchen appliance. The twist we put on with Jay with the juicer initially was that it wasn't a kitchen appliance, it was a health device. It was something that would enable you to live longer, to live healthier. And so this little product that you see that's making the fresh juice basically has amazing health benefits. And we never talk about the size of the motor, you always talk about features versus benefits. We never talked about the blade or the size of the motor or the type of plastic or how much juice it would make. It would be, we included information booklets, the immune strengthening diet, the weight loss juice diet, the anti-aging juice diet. These are all huge benefits that come out of this little machine. Meanwhile, crops and brawn and the other juicer manufacturers were marketing as a kitchen appliance. And so that's a good example of finding a unique selling proposition on the juicer. The toothbrush, the Sonicare wasn't the first electric toothbrush on the market. I think there was a product out there called Interplac. And at the time, all of the products out in the marketplace, the most expensive toothbrush, aside from the Interplac toothbrushes were, I don't know what they cost, $1 or $2. Sonicare was $150. So the challenge was how are we going to sell $150 toothbrush when really nobody understood Sonic technology and what it did? So what we did is really make a message that was centered around, one, understanding what gum disease was and then how this product could help reverse gum disease. So again, we're not talking a lot about the technological advance as much as we're talking about the benefit to the consumer that they couldn't find somewhere else. And that's a good example. So still, grills have been around forever, all sorts of different types of grills. And there's a funny story about the George Foreman grill, Salt and the company that bought our juicer business. Basically, it was a product that they had on the shelf and it was originally a taco maker. And the reason it was slanted the way it was was that they would put ground beef in it, put a taco shell underneath, and then they had a little paddle that they would sweep the ground beef into the taco shell. What we did, it's funny, and it didn't have any sales, not surprisingly. I knew the product had been around, but I didn't know the taco thing. Oh, yeah, that's what it was originally. And the key to that was twofold. It was one, attaching it to George at the time because George is a very likable fellow. But to be honest, revealing some behind the scenes secrets, George hadn't even used the grill when we made the first infomercial with him. The first time he walked on the set was the first time he had actually used it. But the key to that was a very simple thing, was very close up photos of the grease and fat running off of whatever you were cooking so that we come up with the slogan, Knock Out the Fat. And again, it had a health benefit. And the grill, and the other part that we did was a speed and convenience. And that was one of the biggest things was that you could cook fish or burgers or whatever it was meal you were cooking in about half the time because of the two-sided cooking. So it's always, and there's always, to answer your question Dan, there's always a unique way of looking at a product that features a specific benefit. And that's really what you need to do is look at it from a consumer's viewpoint and what is the benefit or what is the unique benefit that your product or service can provide to them. And then really go out and find ways of testing that and getting some consumer response. And then once you do and you hit on it, then you can grow something from a small to very, very large. Alright, so now let's talk about the camera a little bit. Since you went there and you got me with, there's no difference in the camera, just the mounts. And obviously, apparently a target market. Yeah, well the GoPro camera is probably, even at this point I'd say it's probably going to surpass as far as a success. The George Forman Grill is as big as the grill is and still is. I think there's over 30 million units sold because the camera, and it's interesting because if you take this camera, anybody can go make the camera, the technology is there. What the founder of the company did, a guy named Nick Woodman, was basically figure out how to mount it with a wristband first initially and then a chest strap and then a helmet mount and then eventually a surfboard mount so that it tied in to all of the extreme action sports that are out there. And that's how he started marketing it at trade shows. The other part that tied into this was the current technology and social media, which is Facebook. Our nickname for the GoPro camera is the Narsacam because people like to take pictures of themselves and post it on Facebook. And so what we did with the GoPro camera and what they do very well as a company is use user-generated footage, make zillions of YouTube videos and offer an award for the best video. And it has just taken off through the social media channels amazingly. What we've helped do is bring to the table the television. And this isn't a classic direct response ad where you see the camera and then there's an 800 number and you can order it. Basically, everything that these ads do is user-generated footage of people jumping off a cliff or motorcycles or scuba diving or surfing. And there's a tag on the beginning that just announces it's a GoPro, user-generated footage. And at the end there's a contest that if you go to their website, somebody wins one of everything they make every single day. So it drives huge amounts of traffic to their website. Now this is a concept that can be used at any level, at any small level, and whether it's with TV or radio or whatever, as a way of driving traffic to the website and having a giveaway at the end of it is just a very simple direct response or marketing concept. They've just put a little twist on it and have had amazing success. You're also, many people on the call will be familiar with the like, this exercise regimen is so difficult it will kill you, product P90X, right? That's like a huge hit. Yeah, I wasn't involved in the actual, my company, Cesare Direct, we do, it's a marketing agency and we make the commercials and we also buy media time. We place media for the P90X commercial but we didn't actually make any of the spots for it. But to answer your question, yes, that's a huge, huge success out in the marketplace. And of all of these, when you think about the entire range of everything you've been involved with, what do you think are like the critical elements for a direct response success? What do you, when it walks in the door, almost regardless of whether you're going to buy media for it, you're going to produce for it, you're going to work on it, what peaks your interest or what causes you to shake your head and say no hope? There's a couple things. I always look for a product and we get, just like you do, I'm sure, lots and lots of products and people that come to us with inventions and things. And the very first thing I look for is any amount of sales or consumer response to the product, because that means the idea is already validated. It's much easier to market a product and you had mentioned one earlier, Dan, I think you called it Oxy Fresh but it's Oxy Clean. And everyone remembers Billy Mays and the fantastic pitch guy on TV. But we started working with Oxy Clean when they were doing less than a million dollars in sales. This was 1996 and it was the Apple family out of Denver, Colorado. And the only way they were making sales was Billy was on home shopping and having some success selling the product there. So, and then we were able to take what he was doing on home shopping obviously and turn it into a very large direct response marketing success. But it goes back to, I don't care if somebody has had success with a product selling on a local through, like we talked earlier, trade shows, state fairs. If there's some consumer response and actual sales, that's the first thing that gets my interest. The second is different categories and, you know, to name a cleaning products, like housewares, anything that helps, different kitchen appliances, if it's something new and unique, exercise and fitness, beauty and skin care, you know, the area, you can call it get rich quick or motivation, you know, courses that teach people how to make money. And we've worked in all these categories and so a couple things is, one, I look for a category that has had some traditional success over the years in either direct mail, direct response on the internet. And then secondly, I look for a product that actually has that validation or consumer feedback. And those are kind of general parameters. You know, there's a lot more and there's always exceptions to the rules, but those are two of the biggest things that we look for. Well, let's go to the specific promise of the book, getting people to buy now. Now when we build a direct response pitch, what are the key elements people ought to keep in mind about immediate and direct response? Well, there's a few things. One, we like to create something that they can't get anywhere else. It's either a bigger offer, better offer, more enticing offer, so that they can't go like if you have a product at retail, you can compare it to all the other products on the shelf. So we like to have a unique offer. We like to build a lot of perceived value into the product offering. You know, we talked about the juicer. We included a lot of information, booklets that are very inexpensive to make, but they have big promises like I was talking about, the weight loss juice diet, the anti-aging juice diet. So we build a package that has a lot of perceived value, and then really just from a pricing standpoint, believe it or not, we always like to try and test and go out at the highest price possible, which as your business grows enables you to effectively market through different channels. So when we're building that offer, we're basically creating a lot of perceived value and then building it in a way where it's almost like you keep adding on and you get this and you get this, and I know that's kind of a sometimes, but wait, there's more kind of mentality, but we found over the years that if done correctly, it entices the consumer to actually pick up the phone. Dan, you know this being in the business we're in. We don't have a business and why I love this type of advertising versus what we call conventional or traditional advertising because we can measure how effectively our ads work because if nobody picks up the phone and nobody goes online, you haven't done a good job, but on the other hand, you can take that feedback and make changes to the commercial to actually get people to respond. And that's the key to everything we do is getting that response. So in a few minutes we'll let folks get in and pose questions to you or you and I and let them play a little bit. Before we do that, for those who have not yet followed instructions and gone and gotten their pause on the book, you want to give them a quick buy now pitch for the book. And if you have a website or anything you want to give out, now's the time to do it. Okay, I appreciate that. Like Dan had said earlier, the book Buy Now is available at any of the major bookstores or on Amazon. You can order it online. And the website for our company is Cesare Direct, that's C-E-S-A-R-I-D-I-R-E-C-E-C-T dot com. And it talks a lot about the different products we were talking about today and a lot of case studies on there. And it's just a good source of information, you know, following up on a lot of the things that I talked about today. And kind of the feedback we've been getting on the book is a lot of inventors are reading it to get ideas on how to market their product. We talk a lot on the subject matter that Dan, you brought up, how to structure offers. It's really a combination of case studies, stories, a lot of interesting information on these products you've used or heard about, along with real specific how-to information. So it's a kind of a combination. I've got a lot of feedback to the very interesting book to read in addition to the information that's in there. So I hope you, if you do get it, that you read it and enjoy it and feel free to ask any questions then. All right, Tommy, if you want to let people know how they can get in and play, and then we'll start taking folks who want to get in the game with us. Okay, well ladies and gentlemen, at this time, please press star zero on your phone now to get in the queue with me for a question. Once again, hit star zero if you have a question. And Dan, we have our first question. Sean, please go ahead. Thanks for the call. My name is Sean Buck and we sell done-for-you marketing systems to restaurant owners among a few other products. So I know this is B2B and we've talked a little bit B2C, but I'm sure, or I know a lot of it, you know, crosses over. And I guess my question is, is when selling in that environment, you know, do you guys, how do you guys feel we're trying to craft a direct response campaign for them? Do you guys feel that crafting it lead generation-wise or just going straight to a pitch for them is best? And if it is lead generation, what, any ideas on what kind of lead magnet, you know, I guess, free report or CD or what seems to work best? Dan, you want to go ahead and... I'll go first if you like. Sure. And perhaps some clarification for you. So you guys are providing marketing services that bring bodies in the door to restaurants of all kinds across the country. Correct. Yes. And in your own marketing efforts to get clients, what are you doing now? We're getting a lot of...we've got sales. We'll have the sales people who will go cold, basically, right, which obviously sucks. And then we get a lot of referrals and then we get a lot of people asking us just to do more. But you don't have a funnel at this point is what it sounds like. No, it's... You're still trying to figure that out. Yeah. Yeah. Well, you know, first of all, it's sort of a broad comment. A formula like Rick described where you don't necessarily need a unique product, but you do need a unique offer. And then the more irresistible, the better in order to now drive leads. And so if you think about your question and you think about your business situation, you really have...you really have two things you need to accomplish in general. One, you need to get restaurant owners to willingly step forward, raise their hand, and express interest. Because we know that not all restaurant owners are truly interested in and open-minded toward new ways of doing business and getting customers. So you need that to happen. And then secondly, you need a mechanism of converting them to buyers once they do so. In a complex sale where you're not selling a thing, so you're selling services, not here's a course on how to promote your restaurant. In a complex sale, more often than not, my advice would be lead generation, lead capture, and then some sort of presentation. So be that webinar or tele-seminar or setting up the appointment for the boots on the ground to go in if you have that capability, or driving them into preview seminars, into road shows, or whatever. But what I would encourage you to really think about related to this call is the bait. You know, is the offer to those restaurant owners that will bring them to you in the first place, that is so well-crafted, that it attracts the people with the right kind of attitude to be clients, and is irresistible, so they have to pay attention and they have to come forward. Dan, I'd want to add one thing to that. I agree with everything you said. One of the big components of anything we do is good testimonials. We can never have a successful product if the product itself doesn't work or isn't successful. If it is successful, if it's a good product, you'll generate happy users. So in this case, if you have one really good success story of a restaurant where you've driven business and they've reaped the rewards of it, I would really get packaged something, in addition to everything Dan said, around a success story and say, hey, look at what we did for them. We can do this for you as well. And you can get a lot of mileage out of something like that. Well, that's what we've been doing. The response just hasn't been great. So we have some success stories and we've got one chain that we're getting between 28 and 34% redemption rate on. But it hasn't been lead generation. It's basically we've gone straight to, hey, look at what we've done for them and we can do the same for you. And we're getting not great response. And I guess, so I'm thinking, my question is, are you saying after we lead generate, after we get them interested, then we go into the success stories, hey, here's what we do, or maybe we need to tweak our success stories and our whole letter and our campaign that we're running with now to make that work. Did that make sense? Well, look, it may be all of those things, but it just, it sounds to me like you're trying to get across too big of a canyon in one jump. Okay. That's what it sounds like to me. Okay. You know, when you, back to Rick's point, you know, when you have, and you have to be careful about, see, 24% to 36% if those were the numbers or redemption, those are that, like that's a real meaningful statement to you, me and to Rick. In fact, Rick's probably amazed. But to Joe Blow, who owns two pizza shops, he doesn't have a frame of reference for that. Gotcha. And so, earlier, you know, Rick said, you got to present this from the consumer perspective. So, you got to really present this in a way that whoever your target prospect is, whether that's a sole proprietor or somebody that owns five, whoever that is, that they get it. And then you really can't be, you can't be leaping from firsthand raise to signing a contract to have services performed and advertising placed and, you know, all that in one big leap. You're asking for a bigger commitment than buying a book or a home study course or a project. And as to where and when you use testimonials, I think you probably use them at every stage of the game, both to get a hand raised without specificity and then to demonstrate the efficacy of what you do with specificity. Have you done any B2B, Rick, or you almost, other than- Yeah, very, you know, to be honest with you- This is B2B, obviously. Yeah, when we develop new business, we have to do B2B marketing and really what we do is, and I think that what you said is a middle step that, and again, we're not working- I need to know all the information, all the details to really come up with a real specific answer that's going to actually, you know, help you out. But we do B2B marketing for our own business, and that's the way we do it, is really lead with case studies and success stories. But then after somebody, you know, that's a lead gen. After somebody comes in the door, we have a whole package of information that talks about the specific things we're going to do, the benefits of the customer, the step-by-step, and before we actually sit down and talk to that person, and it's for us, it's because we're selling something, you know, when somebody hires our company, they can be spending anywhere from $2,000 to $500,000 to get involved, that there are a lot of steps in between before the actual contract is signed. But we get the initial lead. You know, Dan had asked me about how the book was doing, and it's doing well, but it's really good for new business development, and that was one of the reasons we wrote the book was to get out on, you know, PR around the country. People would hear us, they would contact our company, we would evaluate the product, then we have a whole bunch of stages before we actually have that face-to-face meeting and close the deal. So there's many stages to closing a large B2B sale like that, but it all starts with the contact and the lead gen, and then following up with the correct support materials before the actual closing of the deal. And that's what we're going after, is the larger, more larger restaurants, less small-man pops. Well, you ain't going to get there in one big jump. Yeah, that's the other thing. I hear all the time someone wants to say, you know, they have a great product. Oh, I want to put it on Oprah. I know that show isn't on the air anymore. And they forget that the way you get to Oprah is you do a little radio show, then you do a bigger radio show, and then you do a local TV show, then you do a national show, and after you have all that success, maybe if you're lucky you'll get on a show like Oprah. And so I know your target is the big national chains, but the more little success stories you have and building up and create that solid base to work from, the more success you're going to have with the bigger guys. All right, we're going to have to move on. Take our next question. Thank you. Here are our next questions from Brody. Please go ahead. Hey, that's me. My name is Brody Tyler. Can you hear me okay? Yep, we can hear you. All right, I have so many questions, but I will pick one. I know you've got George Foreman to endorse the grill, and it seemed like it was nothing until he put his name on it, literally. What does it take to get a celebrity endorsement, whether it's for a product or service, or a forward for a book that's being written? How do you approach that? And what type of is it profit sharing going on? What are the details of that arrangement? Yeah, it's a great question, and I do a lot of this on every different level. And the George Foreman, I think until recently, there was a good answer to a trivia question. What was the largest sports endorsement ever? And it was really what George paid, got paid when they bought him out of George Foreman's grill, or bought his royalty stream out, or profit sharing out, and he got paid $130 million for that. But when they did the deal, basically, they didn't pay George anything. It was a situation where George had, after he lost that fight in Zier to Muhammad Ali, he had been through a ten-year stretch where he just got fat and out of shape, and he was making a comeback, and he didn't have very many endorsements because his reputation in the old days, and he says this in his own book, he was kind of a thug, nobody really wanted to endorsement, so he was an easy guy to approach to make a deal, and there was no money up front to change tans. So they signed George Foreman for literally nothing, but they did a profit sharing deal, and obviously it worked out very well for George. So that's one thing to keep on your radar. When we look for people to represent certain infomercials or be hosts or things like that, I always look for people that had had a lot of fame at one time, but maybe are on the downward side of their career, but people still have fond memories of them and things like that. Those are people that can be signed a little bit cheaper, and there's all sorts of ways of doing it, and a profit sharing way is one where it doesn't cost a lot of money up front, but you could be paying a lot of money if your product is real successful, but it's better to have that success and pay that big fee than not have any success at all. Then there's other ways with celebrities that just won't talk to you unless there's cash up front, and a lot of it is a combination of cash and royalties from sales. The other thing, when a lot of the times we make these shows, we get a lot of experts that, whether they're medical doctors or are different book authors, and we just approach them, and you ask how you get these people, I just always pick up the phone and try and track them down and put in a phone call because any exposure, if they're involved in your project and it's a good product, they're going to get exposure, and that helps everybody. If somebody's a book author, they want to be in our infomercials for free. We never pay anybody to be in those, any of the experts we use, because they want the exposure. So there's all sorts of different ways of structuring the deals. It's just limited to the creativity, and don't assume that all these people want a lot of money, they might, but there's ways of structuring it, whether you have to pay very little money up front, and it's based on the success of the project that they can make it big. What's in it for them is the exposure? Well, in some cases, I mean, if you have, so like for a client some years ago, I got Art Linkletter, and I got Art at a lower price than we should have, but the client marketed to seniors, Art had a new book out with Mark Victor Hansen, aimed at that market, and so our giving away books in all the outlets, promoting the book in all the ads, was the same as currency, and it allowed us to get a much lower fee arrangement with Art. So, I mean, Rick's point about creativity, a point I always make to people is, when you set out to go down this path, you should never set out with locked in on a person, but more a category that will be useful to you. So, might be an athlete, might be a sitcom mom, might be because on any one given day, two of them that are almost interchangeable, one will have just come out of a good paying gig and will be quoting some astronomical fee and won't want to talk to you, and the other one hasn't had a good paying gig in five years and has a kid's college tuition payment due, and they're a lot more malleable. The highest rate of unemployment in any occupation in America is in entertainment. And so, generally there is somebody for every need and every budget who can be found and had and be useful, and also in many cases a client will start with, let's say, somebody from the D-list and have enough success that two years later, they can move up to somebody on the B-list. At Gavi Rancor, we launched Proactive. Proactive today, there's Justin Bieber and Katy Perry and the youngest Kardashian, whatever her name is. Yeah, that's a great point. They didn't start there though. We started with Judith Light. Yeah, who did who's that? The sitcom mom for Who's the Boss, after Who's the Boss had been off the air for five years. So, you know, there's almost always somebody, and in many cases you may have other value to offer besides money. Now, the mechanics of getting to them, the first best way is always the back door. And so I tell people all the time to really think about the Kevin Bacon game, you are usually no more than three to five connections away from somebody who would be useful to you. So, you got to rack your brain about who you know and who they might know and who they might know, because a direct phone call saying, hey, Kennedy's a good guy and he'll shoot straight with you, and he wants to talk to you about X is infinitely better than the front door. But there are front doors. There are celebrity brokers who represent buyers, kind of like buyers, agents in real estate. And all of that now is more than we're going to take time for on this call. But if you are really sort of serious about pursuing this and you fax me at my office or get through to customer service at GKIC, I'll be happy to send you some more information that would be helpful to you. Thank you. I appreciate that. And next, I want to be mindful of the clock and Rick's time. I have to jump off in about nine minutes. We're going to lose you in nine. They're going to keep me for another 20. And so do we have another one, Tommy? Yes. Our next question is from Dwight. Please go ahead. So I'm going to assume that it's still a good idea to have your own TV show for your local business. So, Mr. Cesare, what would be the key elements to have on, let's say, if it was a weekly or even bi-weekly TV show for a local business? Success stories I know is one. But should it always be success stories or whatever elements could we put in there? When you say your own local TV show, do you have a specific business that you're talking about? Because when I talk about TV shows, ours aren't regular television shows. Our direct and response commercials and they're either 30 minute commercials or one minute spots. I should have clarified. I'm actually talking about an Internet TV show. Oh, okay. Obviously, and again, this is an area that I have a lot of clients getting into and we do some as well. It has to be really the category and again, I'm just picking something that is in there, whether it's fishing or whatever. This is actually martial arts, if that helps. Oh, okay. Well, then to me, the more guests you can have, use what works on regular TV, okay? If you see something on regular TV, if it's in the martial arts area, I would try to get as many interviews and people that can come on to your show that can bring something, information outside of what you provide on a regular basis. And it's nice to maybe create the show in a way where there's recurring segments, sort of like a newsletter format or an editorial format for a magazine. There's a segment that obviously if it's your show featuring something that you do and a tip of the week, you might have a new guest on every week. You might have a success story. You might have a kid that's taken martial arts and why this is great for kids because it teaches them discipline and respect. Think up different recurring categories and then the challenge is just making sure that you get new and fresh information each and every week or day that you do the show. And Dan, I just found out my Kevin Bacon numbers too. Oh, is that how far away you are? Yep. From him or from someone you want? From him. Oh, well, there you go. What is that, a client, a student? No, actually, I was in a movie with a guy who was in a movie with him. Well, there you go. Unfortunately, that's probably not really useful to you, but I'll keep it in mind. You never know what I might want to get to Kevin Bacon. There you go. And now I know I'm just three away. Ah! See? Good point. All right. All right, Dwight, thanks. Thanks much. Let's try and squeeze in one more real quick. Next question we have is from Bob. Please go ahead. Hi, Dan and Rick. Thank you very much. My question changed midway through. I came in a little late. It's with the by now versus lead generation. I'm the NLP guy. And we've been selling NLP training online successfully for three years. His little snag this year because people come to our site. It surprises how well it worked. People come to the website, see that we offer NLP training online, buy it. Or they get, if they don't buy it right away, they get a well-written Dan Kennedy style sequence that goes out with a sales pitch letter starting within 14 days they buy it. That was doing about 25% of the people coming in. And getting people in obviously is what we're struggling with. Now everyone says, hey, I've got to do lead generation. So we did a big thing in the spring to change over to lead generation. Apparently I don't know how to do that because we stopped getting income. So is there a trick that I don't know that has a shortcut in the process? Because we don't want to stop people from buying now when they come in, but lead generation on that front end now is interrupting the buying now process. Why on earth do you stop doing something that was converting at 25%? Can I charge you for questions? Well, because we wanted more people and I figured, okay, we're only getting so many people coming in. That's pretty good, but we wanted to increase the income. So I thought maybe we can somehow convert the ones that are getting away. Okay. Rick, you want to play? Well, when you talked about the title of the book, Buy Now, it's a little bit of a misnomer because yes, we want people to buy now, but if we're doing a lead gen, the whole purpose of direct response is getting people to take an action. And the lead gen is a huge part of our business. And so sometimes it's the two-step buy now. And so what all we're trying to do with our advertising thing and any program we do is whether it's lead gen or advertising, they're either purchasing now or they're responding to our message and then making the purchase secondary. Again, my first reaction was the same as yours, Dan. It sounded like there was a model that was working very well and then you changed it and what you changed it to isn't working. So I don't know. I would go back to what was working and then don't make such a drastic change. Again, without knowing more of the specifics, it's hard to give a really good answer to that. Right. I caught on to the go back to what we were doing after a couple months. What you should be setting up is a 25% convert with that process. 75% obviously do not convert with that process. Which by the way is an enviable, I'm sure Rick would agree, online conversion, first percentage. So the real question now is not to get in the way of the 25%, but what can we do with the 75% that is more effective after the first yes-no? Because they have real value. Now if even asking them is driving away huge numbers that you are dividing by 25 and 75, then you really want two separate marketing campaigns driving into two separate places that function differently. But you damn sure do not want to get in the way of the 25% who are willing to say yes. Yeah, and I would spend a lot more time figuring out how to drive more people into that funnel because it's working and spending time basically figuring out different ways to drive more people into the closing process that's already working. Right. Okay. So there's no magic. That was just like, okay. No, you really haven't. I agree with what Rick just said. See, we all try and get, you get to a winner in pieces. So you have to conquer, how can I get lead flow at an affordable cost? How can I convert leads? How can I monetize the unconverted leads? In some cases, not yours. How can I move from direct response to forced retail distribution, which is something that Rick's very good at. So you get there in pieces. So you now have a piece that works. So you got a funnel that works and it converts. So long before I'd be monkeying around with that, I'd be trying to get the other pieces up to par, which the best piece to get up to par now is how can we drive 10 times more traffic? Right. Right. I mean, that's the game here. All right. I got to cut you off and I think I have to let Rick go. Thank you very much, sir, for your time and participation. Great. I really appreciate the opportunity. Hey, Rick. Hey, Dan, how are you? I'm good. Thank you, Atch, at London, Ontario, Canada. Ah, the auto repair guy. Yes. I have bought that book, read the book, and a couple things have happened recently that kind of made sense with this book, reading it at this time. We did a direct mail with a really good offer, a lot of offers to check a lot of things on people's cars, change their oil, do a series of tests. It was a really well laid out letter. It didn't perform. The sales letter didn't work. We got offered by Groupon to do an offer through them. So we put the same offer with them. They did a lousy job writing it up, and it performed well. A lot of people bought that, and it has placed cars in our shop and people bought. They actually got a list of what was wrong with their car, got some time spent, which they bought the Groupon for, and they saw good value in it, and then in turn made a purchase. Well, the first thing I will say is that not all Groupon experiences are as happy as yours, and so congratulations. I plan that. I'll tell you how in a minute. Yeah, because you clearly had a very good sales and conversion process in place. Yes. A lot of people don't, and they get brutalized as a result. But what it tells you is that the people to whom the offer is directed matter a lot. And so you have value shoppers to whom you put a great value in front of. Whereas your direct mail, lots of ways for Miss Fire, of course, but one is that that's a different audience. And so the heavy value offer may in fact actually work against you rather than for you in a colder, broader, fuzzier list or environment. And so you may want to, you may have gone so far, for example, as to have raised skepticism and harm trust. You may have de-emphasized other reasons to choose you versus other options other than the value offer. So who we get in front of with each message matters a lot. Specific to your TV question, I absolutely would be driving to phone and web. I think it's too early to drive just to web. But the more you can drive to web, the better, because you can do more there with them. I can do my testimonials there and I can keep them there. So the order in which you present the response instruction now is web first, phone second. And ideally you create some kind of instant gratification incentive, a reason to go to the web that is not connected to the phone, which may very well be. They go to the web and see a four and a half minute video about X or call. And obviously you can't see the video if you don't go to the web. Because you want to wait to traffic there if you can. Now, since I'll let you work, so for everybody's benefit, tell me about your conversion process in place for your Groupon traffic. I simply put in my Groupon, must have appointment. And secondly, I just increased the offer so much that it takes a whole day for the car to stay here to get it. So I could actually control the flow. And I just made it all about controlling how many would come and making it more of a value for them if they leave the car for the entire day. And quite honestly, if the car is here for the entire day, there's a better chance I got a chance to sell something. So I just made sure that, I played with the free oil change before and that's a toe-tapping every 20 minutes. Somebody's wanting to get out of your shop and get going and get what they got for free. I thought if I increase the offer and control the flow, I can do a better job and increase the value for them. And it worked really well through Groupon. Take care. Okay, next question we have is from Dwight Montgomery. Please go ahead. Hey, Dan. How are you doing this afternoon? Real good, you. Super, thanks. Hey, thanks a lot for organizing, Rick. That was very informative. I've got two questions. I have heeded your advice and I refuse to become commoditized in the proverbial tomato. Although our industry, you know, I'm in the office furniture industry. All you have to do is walk into staples or any of the box stores and that's what you find. So what I've set out to do is to go the other direction and create a chair that is designed specifically for overweight Americans. I'm looking at trends and I'm seeing the McDonald's and Krispy Kreme aren't going anywhere and the obesity rate is over the top. And what I'm suggesting is that there are chairs that are not made with cheap plastic. There are chairs that can be produced with metal and that are not going to sink to the floor when sat upon. So I'm looking at it from a trend basis, A. But I guess my two questions are as follows. And I've been in the office furniture industry successfully for 18 years. So I have a track record. We probably sell, I don't know, $800,000 or $900,000 worth of chairs on an annualized basis, but not this chair. This particular prototype that I'm working on. So with that as the context in the backdrop, I'm wondering, do you have a source that you would recommend and your general overview thinking of what a business plan surrounding that would encompass? You know, the direct response market is where I think this has play, but that's atypical from the typical business plan and planners that I've worked with. So do you have a resource to work with in that regard? And then B, I had a great conversation with Fred Couton who said, you know, because I was asking him what made him different, what differentiated that product launch. He said that was simple. He said I gave people a choice. So as I pondered on that, it was like, well, you know what, I may be missing the real boat here, which is why not create a chair that gives people a choice because right now your choice is you go sit down on it and there's a feel right or not. But it's genuine, you know, there are health concerns. If you're six foot four and the next guy's five foot eight and the same chairs is designed to sit on the back of their leg, it cuts off circulation. There's a bunch of research that's out there to support that. So I guess my question to you is, A, do you have a group that you would recommend working with as far as business planning is concerned? B, what are your initial cut response to, do you go after, you know, bigasschairs.com or do you go after, you know, you choose your chair based upon, you know, very similar to a custom suit, you know, I'm six foot two, my end scene is X and I weigh 209 pounds. I'm from a marketing standpoint. I'm not sure one precludes the other, but I am sort of knee jerk reacted to the idea of customization, what you're in scene, what you're weight, that's interesting. There are plus size furniture companies. There's a very high end one that advertises just western decor furniture. It's all plus size. There are plus size bed manufacturers. And so you're not the first to identify the need, the trend, the customer. You may very well be the first one in office chairs. I certainly have not heard about it. You are well aware that there are fairly pricey, ergonomic and adjustable chairs now sold and sold by direct response. There's one that advertises in the New Yorker magazine every month. That is a lead generation ad and there's a website and it's a fairly expensive chair. So I think there is a direct marketing play here for you, assuming you have the right margins. There's a direct marketing play here for you. I think there's still a regular distribution play here for you as well. It has to do with who to refer you to. No, I don't. But I would tell you, so part of my approach, if I would be in your position or to begin working on your behalf, is I would go look for comparables for business models now of successes in customized anything, in stuff for overweight people, anything, in back comfort seating, anything. Cesare has a client called Backjoy Orthotics. I did a little bit of consulting with them early and they're a company worth looking at. The real high priced ergonomic chairs and I can't for life be thinking the name of it, but it's in the New Yorker magazine every month. Somebody got me one as a gift so I happen to have one in my office. So I would find existent businesses that are doing a lot of the things I would need to do, are in a comparable category, are selling to the same consumer, are selling some item at a much higher price than the average in the industry because it is whiz bang and special. So I would find my five or six models to really build my business strategy backwards from. That's where I'd start and that's where I would tell you to start. So what's your knee jerk reaction as to why that particular furniture manufacturer that's advertising in the New Yorker, and I'm not seeing any infomercials, any George Foremans, any William the refrigerator Perry has a… because it's viewed still as a business purchase rather than a consumer purchase, which you know the plethora of home offices, I mean the very fact that he's in the New Yorker and he's not in business week, it tells you he's drifted in that direction. But I would imagine most people look at it and view it as too narrow, you know, when you really broadcast. So a TV infomercial, a mainstream radio spot. Now I think you could do radio but you'd have to be selective. You know Limbaugh has the highest percentage of business owner, listeners of anything on the air. Could you do Limbaugh? Could you do Beck maybe? Could you do broad based radio just buying main radio buys? No, I don't think so. So it would probably be looked at as too narrow. Now the plethora of home offices would suggest maybe otherwise. But the fact that nobody has tried it doesn't mean that it can't be done. It's just as a product nobody has decided to put the muscle behind and try and test on a big mainstream basis. But the very fact that they can be with an ergonomic office chair in the New Yorker, which is an affluent skewed publication, but not necessarily a business skewed publication, says a lot and suggests that that play may very well exist.
